topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I traced the elegant script, the names, the dates, and a sudden, cold dread began to coil in my stomach. Every sentence was a carefully constructed narrative of my life, yet it was utterly hollow where my own experiences should have been. My hands started shaking, the ink blurring slightly under the tremor. I reread a passage describing a pivotal moment in my youth, a memory I distinctly recalled, and a sharp, breathless sound escaped my throat. I crumpled the document, the sound of the tearing paper loud in the quiet room, and walked to the window, staring blankly at the indifferent cityscape, unable to settle my gaze on anything.

[story 2]

It hit me like a physical blow when I saw the annotated timeline. The neat, precise entries detailing my career trajectory, my supposed triumphs, were all meticulously crafted, devoid of the messy, unpredictable reality I lived. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I sank onto the edge of the antique desk, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and thin. I started pacing the small study, my footsteps echoing hollowly, my breath coming in shallow, rapid gasps. Each word in the fabricated history seemed to mock me, a cruel, invisible judgment pressing down on my chest until it felt impossible to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

When the file was finally opened, the sheer volume of fabricated detail overwhelmed me. My eyes scanned the pages rapidly, a frantic search for any anchor point, any genuine thread to pull on. A tightness seized my throat, making swallowing difficult, and I had to stop myself from retching. I dropped the papers onto the floor, the sound muffled by the thick rug. I stood there for a long moment, breathing in ragged, uneven pulls of air, my entire body vibrating with a profound sense of dislocation. I looked around the room as if expecting the walls themselves to reveal the deception, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars.
